content.text: A Claude Code plugin is a directory that bundles skills, hooks, MCP server configurations, settings, and CLAUDE.md instructions into a single portable unit. Installing a plugin enriches every session in its scope — adding slash commands, wiring lifecycle hooks, connecting external tools, and shaping agent behavior — without requiring each team member to manually assemble the same configuration files.
A plugin is a named directory inside

(user scope). Claude Code reads every plugin in both locations at startup and merges their contents into the active session. The merge is additive: skills from all plugins become available as slash commands and subagent types; hooks from all plugins fire in discovery order; MCP servers from all plugins are registered; CLAUDE.md files are prepended to the session context.
The canonical use case is team configuration sharing. A monorepo can ship a

directory checked into version control. Every engineer who opens Claude Code in that repo automatically gets the team's review agents, audit hooks, and internal MCP servers — no manual setup required.
Without plugins, each team member maintains their own

configuration independently. Skills drift, hooks are forgotten, MCP servers are configured differently per machine. This configuration drift creates subtle differences in how Claude Code behaves across the team — some members have the security scanner hook, others do not; some have the PR reviewer agent, others use a slightly different version. Plugins collapse this into a single source of truth.
: checking a plugin into the repo is the installation. Every developer gets it on the next pull.
: multiple plugins coexist. A repo can have a team plugin and a security plugin; their skills and hooks stack.
: plugins can be published as npm packages and installed with a single
: user plugins apply everywhere; project plugins apply only inside the repo. Personal tools stay personal; team tools stay scoped.
: plugin changes go through the same PR review as code, so the team's AI tooling evolves alongside the codebase.

: when multiple plugins register the same hook event, execution order depends on filesystem discovery order, which is not always predictable. Sensitive hooks (that block or deny actions) need careful testing in multi-plugin setups.
: two plugins that register a skill with the same
field will conflict. Later-loaded plugins shadow earlier ones, which can be surprising.
: all plugins share the same Claude Code process. A hook in one plugin can observe or interfere with commands triggered by another plugin's skill.

: plugin CLAUDE.md files are prepended to the session context before the project CLAUDE.md. If a plugin's instructions conflict with the project's, the project wins — but only because it comes last in the prepend chain. Verify this behavior when instructions are ambiguous.
When Claude Code starts a session it scans for plugins in this order:
Built-in skills and hooks shipped with Claude Code itself.
— apply to every session regardless of project.
— apply only when working inside this repository.
, and top-level skills and hooks (outside any plugin directory).

For each plugin Claude Code reads, it merges the plugin's components into the running session. Skills become callable slash commands and agent types. Hooks are appended to the event chains for their declared events. MCP server definitions are registered. Settings are applied (later scopes win for scalar values; array values like allowed tools are union-merged).

attrs.alt: Plugin architecture: directory structure flows through Claude Code startup loader into runtime capabilities
attrs.title: Claude Code Plugin Architecture

content.text: : any configuration the whole team should share — review agents, audit hooks, internal MCP servers.
: skills or hooks you want available everywhere (user-scope plugin in
: a security team can publish a compliance plugin as an npm package that any project can
: data science teams can bundle pandas/sklearn agents and Python hooks; frontend teams can bundle accessibility checkers and design-system agents.

# Project-scoped plugin (checked into the repo)
mkdir -p .claude/plugins/team-tools

# User-scoped plugin (personal, all projects)
mkdir -p ~/.claude/plugins/my-tools
manifest is optional but strongly recommended. It declares the plugin's identity and may reference npm-packaged plugins.
under the plugin directory. Each file is a self-contained skill with YAML frontmatter:

---
name: code-reviewer
description: Reviews code changes for correctness, style, and security issues.
agentType: code-reviewer
tools:
  - Grep
  - Read
  - Glob
  - WebSearch
---

You are a rigorous code reviewer. Examine every changed file for:
- Logic errors and off-by-one mistakes
- Security vulnerabilities (injection, auth bypass, insecure deserialization)
- Style violations relative to the existing codebase conventions
- Missing error handling at I/O boundaries

Respond with a structured report: summary, then per-file findings.

# File: .claude/plugins/team-tools/hooks/audit-bash.sh
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail
payload=$(cat)
cmd=$(echo "$payload" | jq -r '.tool_input.command // empty')
if echo "$cmd" | grep -qE 'curl.*\|.*sh'; then
  echo '{"hookSpecificOutput":{"hookEventName":"PreToolUse","permissionDecision":"deny","permissionDecisionReason":"curl-pipe-sh pattern blocked"}}'
  exit 2
fi
exit 0
inside the plugin directory is prepended to the session context before the project's own CLAUDE.md. Use it for standing instructions that apply whenever the plugin is active:

<!-- File: .claude/plugins/team-tools/CLAUDE.md -->
# Team Engineering Standards

- All database queries must use parameterized statements. Never interpolate user input.
- New endpoints require an integration test before merge.
- Use the internal Jira MCP server (mcp__internal-jira__*) to create tickets, not freeform notes.

.claude/plugins/team-tools/
├── plugin.json               # manifest (name, version, description)
├── CLAUDE.md                 # standing instructions, prepended to context
├── settings.json             # hook registrations, permissions, env vars
├── mcp.json                  # MCP server definitions
├── skills/
│   ├── code-reviewer.md      # /code-reviewer slash command + agent type
│   ├── security-auditor.md   # /security-auditor slash command + agent type
│   └── doc-writer.md         # /doc-writer slash command + agent type
└── hooks/
    ├── audit-bash.sh         # PreToolUse → Bash
    └── log-edits.sh          # PostToolUse → Edit

For plugins shared across multiple repositories or organizations, publishing to npm decouples the plugin lifecycle from any single repo. The plugin is a standard npm package whose contents are the plugin directory files.
After publishing, a consuming project installs it with npm and links it by path:
Claude Code resolves the path relative to the project root and loads the plugin directory at that location. Updating the plugin then becomes a standard
plus a commit of the lockfile.
A platform engineering team wants every Claude Code session in their monorepo to:
Offer a /pr-review agent that understands their PR checklist
Block any Bash command that writes to production S3 buckets
Make the internal Jira MCP server available in every session
Remind Claude that all service boundaries must use protobufs, not JSON blobs

with a PR reviewer skill, an S3 guard hook, the Jira MCP config, and a CLAUDE.md that states the protobuf rule. They commit and push it. On the next pull, every engineer's Claude Code sessions automatically have all four capabilities — no instructions, no manual config.

# S3 guard hook: .claude/plugins/platform/hooks/guard-s3.sh
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail
payload=$(cat)
cmd=$(echo "$payload" | jq -r '.tool_input.command // empty')
if echo "$cmd" | grep -qE 's3://(prod|production)-'; then
  printf '%s\n' '{"hookSpecificOutput":{"hookEventName":"PreToolUse","permissionDecision":"deny","permissionDecisionReason":"Direct writes to production S3 buckets are blocked"}}'
  exit 2
fi
exit 0
Later plugin silently shadows earlier one
Hook silently skipped or throws EACCES
Registering hooks in plugin.json instead of settings.json
Hook registrations belong in settings.json, not plugin.json
All skill .md files must live in plugin's skills/ directory
: prefix skill names, hook script names, and MCP server names with the plugin name to prevent collisions (e.g.
: run each hook script directly with a realistic payload before checking into the plugin. Untested hooks can silently block legitimate operations.
: write only the standing instructions that the plugin's tools need. Cross-cutting project standards belong in the project's own CLAUDE.md, not in the plugin.
: if the plugin ships as an npm package, use semver so consuming projects can pin a known-good version.
description field and a brief README are the only documentation most users will read. List the slash commands, the hook events protected, and any environment variables required.
Publishable to npm; installable across repos
Skills + hooks + MCP + settings in one directory
Treat plugin code as first-class code
: hook scripts execute with the same permissions as the Claude Code process. Malicious or buggy hooks can read files, exfiltrate data, or deny legitimate operations. Review plugin changes in PRs.
: before installing a third-party Claude Code plugin, inspect its hook scripts and MCP server definitions — the same supply-chain diligence you apply to other npm packages.
) and load secrets from a secrets manager or shell profile.
Hook failures are silent by default
: a hook that throws an unhandled exception is logged but does not abort the tool call. Add explicit error handling and audit logging inside hooks that enforce security rules.
A plugin is a named directory under .claude/plugins/ or ~/.claude/plugins/ that bundles skills, hooks, MCP configs, settings, and CLAUDE.md instructions.
Claude Code merges all active plugins at startup: skills add slash commands and subagent types; hooks append to event chains; MCP servers are registered; CLAUDE.md files are prepended.
Project plugins are checked into version control and shared automatically; user plugins apply personally across all projects.
Plugins can be published as npm packages, enabling organization-wide distribution with standard package management.
Namespace skill and hook names to avoid collisions, keep secrets out of mcp.json, and audit plugin code with the same rigor as application code.
